name: workflow-patterns
description: Use this skill when implementing tasks according to Conductor's TDD
capabilities: Implement phase checkpoints with verification protocols
Manage git commits with structured commit messages and git notes
Enforce quality assurance gates (coverage, style, linting, security)
Handle deviations from original plans systematically
Create verification checklists for automated and manual testing
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analysis detected 80 potential security issues in resources/implementation-playbook.md, all of which are false positives. The file contains Markdown documentation with bash code examples for educational purposes. The external_commands patterns are git command examples in code blocks (lines 25-571), not executable code. The weak_crypto findings are documentation text, not cryptographic implementations. This skill contains only documentation and no executable code, posing no security risk.
valueStatement: Struggling with disorganized development workflow and poor test coverage. This skill provides a structured TDD framework with phase checkpoints, git commit management, and verification protocols to ensure code quality throughout implementation.
limitations: Requires git repository to utilize commit and checkpoint features
Focused on TDD methodology - not suitable for non-test-driven workflows
Documentation-only skill - does not execute commands or enforce rules automatically
Designed for Conductor framework - may need adaptation for other workflows

useCases.title: Implement New Feature with TDD
useCases.description: Developer implements a new user authentication feature following the red-green-refactor cycle, with proper test coverage and git commits at each step.

useCases.title: Establish Phase Checkpoints
useCases.description: Team lead creates verification checkpoints at the end of each development phase to ensure quality gates are met before proceeding.

useCases.title: Track Implementation Progress
useCases.description: Developer tracks task completion by updating plan.md with commit SHAs and maintaining traceability from planning to code.

promptTemplates.title: Start TDD Task Implementation
promptTemplates.prompt: I'm starting Task 2.1: Implement user validation. Help me follow the TDD workflow to write failing tests first, then implement the minimum code to pass, and refactor for clarity.
promptTemplates.scenario: Beginning a new task from plan.md

promptTemplates.title: Create Phase Checkpoint
promptTemplates.prompt: I've completed all tasks in Phase 1. Help me create a checkpoint commit with verification: run full test suite, check coverage above 80%, and generate manual verification checklist.
promptTemplates.scenario: Completing a phase and need checkpoint verification

promptTemplates.title: Format Structured Git Commit
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me create a structured commit message for Task 2.1 completion following the format: type(scope): subject, body with bullet points, and footer with task/track references.
promptTemplates.scenario: Committing completed task with proper format

promptTemplates.title: Handle Implementation Deviation
promptTemplates.prompt: During implementation I discovered we need a different approach than planned. Help me document this deviation properly in plan.md and tech-stack.md.
promptTemplates.scenario: Deviating from original plan during implementation

bestPractices: Always write failing tests first (RED) before implementation (GREEN) - never skip the RED phase
Create atomic commits - one logical change per commit with tests passing after every commit
Wait for explicit user approval before proceeding past phase checkpoints - do not skip verification gates
Update plan.md immediately after completing each task with the commit SHA for traceability
antiPatterns: Writing implementation code before tests - this violates TDD principles and reduces test effectiveness
Batching multiple tasks into one commit - this breaks traceability and makes semantic reversion difficult
Proceeding to next phase without checkpoint approval - this bypasses quality gates and risks accumulating technical debt
Updating plan.md only after multiple tasks - this loses traceability and makes progress auditing difficult

faq.question: What is the RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycle?
faq.answer: RED means write failing tests first. GREEN means write minimum code to pass tests. REFACTOR means improve code structure while keeping tests green. This cycle ensures tests drive development and code stays clean.

faq.question: Why do I need to record commit SHAs in plan.md?
faq.answer: Recording commit SHAs creates traceability from plan to code. It enables semantic revert operations, progress auditing, and helps you understand which code implements which specific task.

faq.question: Can I proceed to the next phase without checkpoint approval?
faq.answer: No. Checkpoint approval is a critical quality gate. Proceeding without approval risks accumulating undetected issues. Always wait for explicit approval before moving to the next phase.

faq.question: What should I do if implementation deviates from the plan?
faq.answer: Document the deviation in plan.md with the reason and impact. Update tech-stack.md if dependencies changed. Update spec.md if requirements changed. This maintains traceability of decisions.

faq.question: Why use git notes instead of putting everything in the commit message?
faq.answer: Git notes preserve rich context without cluttering commit messages. They enable semantic queries across commits and support track-based operations while keeping commits concise and scannable.

faq.question: What is the 80% coverage target and why is it important?
faq.answer: The 80% coverage target ensures comprehensive testing of new code. It balances thoroughness with pragmatism - 100% is often not cost-effective, but below 80% risks missing critical paths.
